\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_20
% title: IdSeparation
\def\rowref{def\_3\_20}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_20}

\begin{Def}[$id$-separation]
    Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $A,B,C \ins J \cup V$ (not necessarily disjoint) subset of nodes.
    We then say that:
    \begin{enumerate}
        \item \emph{$A$ is $id$-separated from $B$ given $C$ in $G$}, in symbols:
    \[ A \idPerp_G B \given C,  \]
    if every walk from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$ (sic!)\footref{fn:why-J} is $C$-$d$-blocked by $C$.
        \item If that property does not hold we will write:
            \[ A \nidPerp_G B \given C.\]
        \item We also define the special case:
                \[ A \idPerp_G B \qquad :\iff \qquad A \idPerp_G B \given \emptyset.\]
        \item For the special case $J = \emptyset$---that is, if $G$ has no input nodes---$id$-separation is also called \emph{$d$-separation}, and we write:
          \[A \dPerp_G B \given C := A \idPerp_G B \given C, \qquad A \ndPerp_G B \given C := A \nidPerp_G B \given C.\]
          % claim_3_29
          \begin{claimmark}
          Note that $d$-separation is symmetric: $A \dPerp_G B \given C \iff B \dPerp_G A \given C$, by the same argument as for $\sigma$-separation.
          \end{claimmark}
    \end{enumerate}
\end{Def}

\end{document}
